era_context: The mid-second millennium BCE, characterized by the dominance of palace-centered Bronze Age states. Egypt's New Kingdom is in its ascendancy under the 18th Dynasty. The Mycenaean civilization in Greece is in its mature phase. The Shang dynasty controls the Yellow River valley in China. The Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating power in Anatolia. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its final urban phase before fragmentation.

events.title: Hatshepsut's regency or early co-regency in Egypt
events.description: Hatshepsut, wife of Thutmose II, is either serving as regent for the young Thutmose III or beginning her co-regency. Her exact role and dating in this period are debated. She will eventually become one of Egypt's most successful rulers, but her early years are poorly documented. This period marks the transition of power within the 18th Dynasty.

sources.name: Later temple inscriptions and biographical texts from Hatshepsut's reign
Egyptian king lists and chronological records

events.certainty_note: Hatshepsut's early regency is poorly documented. Her rise to power is reconstructed from later inscriptions and king lists. Dating her regency to specific years carries ±5–10 year uncertainty.

events.title: Shang dynasty continues oracle bone divination and record-keeping
events.description: The Shang dynasty maintains its sophisticated system of oracle bone divination and administrative record-keeping. Inscribed bones document royal hunts, military campaigns, weather predictions, and religious ceremonies. This practice provides a window into Shang governance, cosmology, and daily concerns.

sources.name: Oracle bone inscriptions from Anyang and other Shang sites

events.certainty_note: Oracle bones are directly dated through archaeological stratigraphy. Specific year assignment carries ±50 year uncertainty due to competing Shang chronologies.

events.title: Mycenaean trade networks active in Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Mycenaean merchants and traders are actively engaged in long-distance trade throughout the Eastern Mediterranean. Mycenaean pottery and goods are found in Egypt, the Levant, and Cyprus. This trade is facilitated by Mycenaean naval power and palace-sponsored commerce. The exchange includes luxury goods, raw materials (tin, copper), and possibly mercenary services.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Mycenaean pottery and goods in Mediterranean sites

events.certainty_note: Mycenaean trade is archaeologically well-attested through pottery distribution and material remains. Specific dating to this year is approximate (±50 years).

events.title: Hittite Old Kingdom military campaigns in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom continues military campaigns to expand and consolidate territorial control in Anatolia. Hittite rulers engage in warfare against neighboring kingdoms and city-states, establishing Hittite dominance in the region. These campaigns are documented in royal inscriptions and archives.
key_figures: Hittite Old Kingdom rulers (specific names uncertain)

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions from Boğazköy archives

events.certainty_note: Hittite Old Kingdom chronology is debated. Specific dating of campaigns to individual years carries ±50–100 year uncertainty.

events.title: Indus Valley urban centers experience continued decline
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its decline phase. Urban centers show reduced population, simplified architecture, and declining craft production. Trade networks with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf are further contracted. The civilization is fragmenting into regional cultures and smaller settlements.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The decline of the Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically documented. Specific year assignment carries ±50–100 year uncertainty.

disconfirming_evidence: Hatshepsut's early career is poorly documented, and her exact role in 1501 BCE is speculative. Some scholars question whether she held formal co-regency at this early date. The causes of the Indus Valley Civilization's decline remain contested; older theories of Aryan invasion are now largely rejected by mainstream scholarship.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1501 BCE is similar to 1500 BCE: heavily weighted toward literate civilizations with surviving written records and toward regions with extensive archaeological investigation. Egyptian and Hittite records are politically motivated. Mycenaean Linear B tablets are administrative rather than narrative. The Indus Valley Civilization's undeciphered script limits understanding. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are severely underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Hatshepsut's early regency eventually leads to her assumption of full pharaonic power
